Thunderstorms moved through the area during the early morning hours.  Damage occurred on a farm 5 miles north and 1 mile east of Manilla.  The damage appeared to be that of a downburst wind with trees downed and some shed damage on the farm.

Thunderstorms moving through the area caused considerable lightning with numerous reports around the county of transformers being hit.  Lightning struck the Lutheran Church in Munterville, setting it on fire.  The fire was quickly contained, however there was considerable smoke damage.

Lightning set the roof of a house on fire in the city of Boone during the evening hours.  The fire was contained fairly quickly, however the fire caused many thousands of dollars damage before it was contained.

An upper level disturbance moved east northeast from the central and southern Rockies into the central plains as surface low pressure strengthened over eastern Colorado.  Freezing rain was widespread across the area causing several telephone outages, power outages, and auto accidents.

Thunderstorms moved across southwest and central Iowa during the afternoon hours.  Lightning struck a tree near New Virginia.  The tree exploded with pieces spread over 125 feet away from the tree.  One cow was killed by flying debris.

A strong low pressure system passed north of Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  A strong Pacific high pressure system moved n following the lows departure.  A tight pressure gradient then existed over Iowa.  Strong northwest winds of 30 to 40 MPH were common over the state with frequent gusts over 50 MPH.  Some of the highest winds affected the central counties.  Winds gusted to 63 MPH in Boone, and 59 MPH in Johnston for example.  Some minor damage was reported around the state.  There were a few reports of more serious damage.  One was in Laurens, in Pochontas County, where 60 MPH winds removed the roof off of a business.  Damage was in the $3000 to $3500 range.  Winds of 65 MPH in the Forest City area of Winnebago County tore roofs off of a building.  Similar winds in the Manning area of Carroll County also damaged roofs of a few buildings there.
Thunderstorms moved through Mahaska County during the afternoon hours.  They were not severe.  Lightning struck a house in Oskaloosa, causing minor injuries to one person.

Marginal severe thunderstorms moved through the Mason City area.  Damage was limited around the city, however there were two injuries caused by downed trees.  Both occurred in a similar fashion.  A tree fell on top of a car, resulting in one injury.  A short time later, a tree fell on top of three cars resulting in another injury.

Thunderstorms developed over the western portion of the state.  In most cases, they were not severe.  One storm produced what appeared to be a downburst on a farm southeast of Wall Lake.  Trees were downed, along with several sheds and a barn.

After major Winter Storms on the 19th and the 26th, a blanket of thick snow lay across the area.  A re-enforcing shot of cold air pushed over the area on the 30th and took temperatures below zero through the first few days of February.  All time record low temperatures occurred at many locations during a 6 day period.  Record lows ranged from -25 to -32 for 2 to 3 nights, with highs reaching only -10 to -15 degrees during the day for 2 to 3 days.  Waine Kirby, president of the Iowa Sheep Industry and Chairman of the Iowa Sheep Advisory Board said:  "this has been the toughest lambing season he has been through."  Due to the extreme cold temperatures trade at the livestock shows was very light and a meat packing plant had to bring in 6,000 hogs to continue production.  The extreme cold also caused insulator failures that left thousands of customers without power.  The crews would restore power in one area only to have another failure elsewhere.  In the Quad Cities 1 man nearly lost his fingers due to exposure while he changed a tire on his car.  A homeless man lost toes due to frostbite after sleeping outside under a bridge.  Other homeless persons were using busses to stay inside and warm.
A 35 year old woman died in the blizzard west of Algona.  She left Algona by car and became stuck in the snow.  In an attempt to find help, she froze to death about 2 1/2 miles west of town.

Due to the extreme cold temperatures trade at the livestock shows was very light and a meat packing plant had to bring in 6,000 hogs to continue production.  The extreme cold also caused insulator failures that left thousands of customers without power.  The crews would restore power in one area only to have another failure elsewhere.  In the Quad Cities 1 man nearly lost his fingers due to exposure while he changed a tire on his car.  A homeless man lost toes due to frostbite after sleeping outside under a bridge.  Other homeless persons were using busses to stay inside and warm.

After major Winter Storms on the 19th and the 26th, a blanket of thick snow lay across the area.  A re-enforcing shot of cold air pushed over the area on the 30th and took temperatures below zero through the first few days of February.  All time record low temperatures occurred at many locations during a 6 day period.
